summary: Jeff Atwood explores the paradox of programming games that simulate tedious, low-level coding—like assembly language—and argues these games reveal something essential about great programmers. He traces the history from brutally difficult games like Robot Odyssey to modern examples like TIS-100 and Human Resource Machine, noting how they filter for people who genuinely enjoy the challenge of programming. Atwood connects this to hiring, suggesting that watching someone play through a programming game reveals more about their abilities than traditional whiteboard interviews. He highlights Starfighter's CTF-style hiring platform as a revolutionary approach and expresses regret that Stack Overflow never implemented yearly programming game events.
key_points: Assembly language programming is universally considered tedious, yet games like TIS-100 turn it into an engaging puzzle by adding mystery and context
The best programmers share a sense of play and experimentation—they bend code to their will out of sheer joy rather than obligation
Programming games like Human Resource Machine could be better interview tools than FizzBuzz because they reveal improvisation and genuine interest
Starfighter's CTF-style simulations represent a next-level hiring strategy that tests real troubleshooting and reverse engineering skills
The willingness to build unnecessary but creative solutions (like a wireless trading device for a game) signals the kind of programmer you want to hire
money_quotes: You save time when you don't need to have an awards ceremony every time a C statement does what it's supposed to.
This is the assembly language programming game you never asked for!
Any kid who completes this game while still a kid is guaranteed a career as a software engineer.
Is this interview about demonstrating competency in a certain technical skill that's worth a certain amount of money, or showing me how you can improvise and have fun?
The best programmers I've known have all had a lifelong passion for what they do. There's no way a minor economic blip would ever convince them they should do anything else.
I've felt for a long time that Stack Overflow should be doing yearly programming game events exactly like this.
An arbitrary programming game, particularly one that goes to great lengths to simulate a fictional system, is a wonderful expression of the inherent joy in playing and experimenting with code.
key_insight: The programmers worth hiring are those who play with code for the joy of it—and programming games that simulate tedious or complex systems are excellent at revealing who those people are.
